questions.question: Why is 'The food was not great' a hard case for naive BoW classifiers?
options: Negation flips meaning, but bag of words discards the link between 'not' and 'great'
questions.explanation: BoW cannot bind 'not' to the word it negates, so the classifier misses the polarity flip.

questions.question: Why does Naive Bayes work despite its 'naive' independence assumption?
options: With sparse text features and moderate data the classifier mostly cares which side each word leans toward, not exact joint probabilities
The assumption is actually true for text
questions.explanation: Even with wrong independence, leaning-direction information per word is enough to classify well.

questions.question: Why does apply_negation prefix tokens with 'NOT_' during preprocessing?
options: To turn 'good' versus 'NOT_good' into separate features that the classifier can weight oppositely
questions.explanation: apply_negation splits negated forms into distinct features so train_nb can assign opposite weights to flipped polarity.

questions.question: Why is removing stopwords risky for sentiment analysis?
options: Negation words ('not', 'no', 'never') are usually treated as stopwords but carry sentiment signal
questions.explanation: Default stopword lists drop negations and similar carriers of sentiment.

questions.question: Which metric should you report when sentiment classes are imbalanced?
questions.explanation: Macro-F1 forces the minority class to count; accuracy or micro-F1 hides it.

questions.question: Why is L2 regularization important for logistic regression on text?
options: Sparse high-dimensional text features otherwise let the model memorize training examples
questions.explanation: L2 prevents overfitting in the sparse-feature, high-dimensional regime of text.
